volumeTitle: Children of Heracles. Hippolytus. Andromache. Hecuba
label: Andromache (Section 905 - Section 1061)

sections.label: Section 905
sections.original: παίδων γυναικὶ συμφορὰ πλὴν εἰς λέχος ;
sections.translation: unless her honour is concerned ?

sections.label: Section 906
sections.original: τοῦτ ’ αὐτὸ καὶ νοσοῦμεν · εὖ μ ’ ὑπηγάγου .
sections.translation: My very complaint ! Thou hast hit my case exactly .

sections.label: Section 907
sections.original: ἄλλην τιν ’ εὐνὴν ἀντὶ σοῦ στέργει πόσις ;
sections.translation: On whom has thy husband set his affections in thy stead ?

sections.label: Section 908
sections.original: τὴν αἰχμάλωτον Ἕκτορος ξυνευνέτιν .
sections.translation: On his captive , Hector ’ s wife .

sections.label: Section 909
sections.original: κακόν γ ’ ἔλεξας , ἄνδρα δίσσ ’ ἔχειν λέχη .
sections.translation: An evil case indeed , for a man to have two wives !

sections.label: Section 910
sections.original: τοιαῦτα ταῦτα . κᾆτ ’ ἔγωγ ’ ἠμυνάμην .
sections.translation: ’ Tis even thus . So I resented it .

sections.label: Section 911
sections.original: μῶν εἰς γυναῖκ ’ ἔρραψας οἷα δὴ γυνή ;
sections.translation: Didst thou with woman ’ s craft devise a plot against thy rival ?

sections.label: Section 912
sections.original: φόνον γ ’ ἐκείνῃ καὶ τέκνῳ νοθαγενεῖ .
sections.translation: Yes , to slay her and her bastard child .

sections.label: Section 913
sections.original: κἄκτεινας , ἤ τις συμφορά σ ’ ἀφείλετο ;
sections.translation: And didst thou slay them , or did something happen to rescue them from thee ?

sections.label: Section 914
sections.original: γέρων γε Πηλεύς , τοὺς κακίονας σέβων .
sections.translation: It was old Peleus , who showed regard to the weaker side .

sections.label: Section 915
sections.original: σοὶ δ ’ ἦν τις ὅστις τοῦδ ’ ἐκοινώνει φόνου ;
sections.translation: Hadst thou any accomplice in this attempted murder ?

sections.label: Section 916
sections.original: πατήρ γ ’ ἐπ ’ αὐτὸ τοῦτ ’ ἀπὸ Σπάρτης μολών .
sections.translation: My father came from Sparta for this very purpose .

sections.label: Section 917
sections.original: κἄπειτα τοῦ γέροντος ἡσσήθη χερί ;
sections.translation: And was he after all defeated by that old man ’ s prowess ?

sections.label: Section 918
sections.original: αἰδοῖ γε · καί μ ’ ἔρημον οἴχεται λιπών .
sections.translation: Oh no ! but by shame ; and he hath gone and left me all alone .

sections.label: Section 919
sections.original: συνῆκα · ταρβεῖς τοῖς δεδραμένοις πόσιν .
sections.translation: I understand ; thou art afraid of thy husband for that thou hast done .

sections.label: Section 920
sections.original: ἔγνως · ὀλεῖ γάρ μ ’ ἐνδίκως . τί δεῖ λέγειν ;
sections.translation: Thou hast guessed it ; for he will have a right to me . What can I say for myself ? Yet I beseech thee by Zeus the god of our family , send me to a land as far as possible from this , or to my father ’ s house ; for these very [*] walls seem to cry out Begone !

sections.label: Section 925
sections.original: μισεῖ τε γαῖα Φθιάς . εἰ δ ’ ἥξει πάρος
sections.translation: and all the land of Phthia bates me . But if my lord return ere that from the oracle of Phoebus , he will put me to death on a shameful charge , or enslave me to his mistress , whom I ruled before . Maybe [*] some one will say , How was it thou didst go thus astray ?

sections.label: Section 930
sections.original: κακῶν γυναικῶν εἴσοδοί μ ’ ἀπώλεσαν ,
sections.translation: I was ruined by mischievous women who came to me and puffed me up with words like these : What ! wilt thou suffer that vile captive , a mere bondmaid , to dwell within thy house and share thy wedded rights ? By Heaven ’ s queen ! if it were my house

sections.label: Section 935
sections.original: βλέπουσ ’ ἂν αὐγὰς τἄμ ’ ἐκαρποῦτ ’ ἂν λέχη .
sections.translation: she should not live to reap my marriage-harvest ! And I listened to the words of these Sirens , the cunning , knavish , subtle praters , and was filled with silly thoughts . What need had I to care about my lord ?

sections.label: Section 940
sections.original: πολὺς μὲν ὄλβος · δωμάτων δ ’ ἠνάσσομεν ·
sections.translation: I had all I wanted , wealth in plenty , a house in which I was mistress , and as for children , mine would be born in wedlock , while hers would be bastards , half-slaves to mine . Oh ! never , never ,– this truth will I repeat ,– should men of sense , who have wives ,

sections.label: Section 945
sections.original: πρὸς τὴν ἐν οἴκοις ἄλοχον ἐσφοιτᾶν ἐᾶν
sections.translation: allow women-folk to visit them in their homes , for they teach them mischief ; one , to gain some private end , helps to corrupt their honour ; another , having made a slip herself , wants a companion in misfortune , while many are wantons ; and hence it is

sections.label: Section 950
sections.original: νοσοῦσιν ἀνδρῶν . πρὸς τάδ ’ εὖ φυλάσσετε
sections.translation: men ’ s houses are tainted . Wherefore keep strict guard upon the portals of your houses with bolts and bars ; for these visits of strange women lead to no good result , but a world of mischief . [*]

sections.label: Section 954
sections.original: ἄγαν ἐφῆκας γλῶσσαν εἰς τὸ σύμφυτον .
sections.translation: Thou hast given thy tongue too free a rein regarding thy own sex .

sections.label: Section 955
sections.original: συγγνωστὰ μέν νυν σοὶ τάδ ’, ἀλλ ’ ὅμως χρεὼν
sections.translation: I can pardon thee in this case , but still women ought to smooth over their sisters ’ weaknesses .

sections.label: Section 957
sections.original: σοφόν τι χρῆμα τοῦ διδάξαντος βροτοὺς
sections.translation: ’ Twas sage counsel he gave who taught men to hear the arguments on both sides . I , for instance , though aware of the confusion in this house ,

sections.label: Section 960
sections.original: ἔριν τε τὴν σὴν καὶ γυναικὸς Ἕκτορος ,
sections.translation: the quarrel between thee and Hector ’ s wife , waited awhile and watched to see whether thou wouldst stay here or from fear of that captive art minded to quit these halls . Now it was not so much regard for thy message that brought me hither ,

sections.label: Section 965
sections.original: εἰ δ ’ ἐνδιδοίης , ὥσπερ ἐνδίδως , λόγον ,
sections.translation: as the intention of carrying thee away from this house , if , as now , thou shouldst grant me a chance of saying so . For thou wert mine formerly , but art now living with thy present husband through thy father ’ s baseness ; since he , before invading Troy ’ s domains , betrothed thee to me , and then [*] afterwards promised thee

sections.label: Section 970
sections.original: τῷ νῦν σ ’ ἔχοντι , Τρῳάδ ’ εἰ πέρσοι πόλιν .
sections.translation: to thy present lord , provided he captured the city of Troy .

sections.label: Section 971
sections.original: ἐπεὶ δ ’ Ἀχιλλέως δεῦρ ’ ἐνόστησεν γόνος ,
sections.translation: So , as soon as Achilles ’ son returned hither , I forgave thy father , but entreated the bridegroom to forego his marriage with thee , telling him all I had gone through and my present misfortune ; I might get

sections.label: Section 975
sections.original: γήμαιμ ’ ἀπ ’ ἀνδρῶν , ἔκτοθεν δ ’ οὐ ῥᾳδίως ,
sections.translation: a wife , I said , from amongst friends , but outside their circle ’ twas no easy task for one exiled like myself from home . Thereat he grew abusive , taunting me with my mother ’ s murder and those blood-boltered fiends . [*] And I was humbled by the fortunes of my house ,

sections.label: Section 980
sections.original: ἤλγουν μὲν ἤλγουν , συμφοραῖς δ ’ ἠνειχόμην ,
sections.translation: and though ’ tis true , I grieved , yet did I endure my sorrow , and reluctantly departed , robbed of thy promised hand . Now therefore , since thou findest thy fortune so abruptly changed and art fallen thus on evil days and hast no help , I will take thee hence and place thee in thy father ’ s hands .

sections.label: Section 985
sections.original: τὸ συγγενὲς γὰρ δεινόν , ἔν τε τοῖς κακοῖς
sections.translation: For kinship [*] hath strong claims , and in adversity there is naught better than a kinsman ’ s kindly aid .

sections.label: Section 987
sections.original: νυμφευμάτων μὲν τῶν ἐμῶν πατὴρ ἐμὸς
sections.translation: As for my marriage , my father must look to it ; ’ tis not for me to decide that . Yes , take me hence as soon as may be ,

sections.label: Section 990
sections.original: μὴ φθῇ με προσβὰς δῶμα καὶ μολὼν πόσις ,
sections.translation: lest my husband come back to his house before I am gone , or Peleus hear that I am deserting his son ’ s abode and pursue me on horse-back .

sections.label: Section 993
sections.original: θάρσει γέροντος χεῖρα · τὸν δ ’ Ἀχιλλέως
sections.translation: Rest easy about the old man ’ s power ; and , as for Achilles ’ son with all his insolence to me , never fear him ;

sections.label: Section 995
sections.original: τοία γὰρ αὐτῷ μηχανὴ πεπλεγμένη
sections.translation: such a crafty net this hand hath woven and set for his death with knots that none can loose ; whereof I will not speak before the time , but , when my plot begins to work , Delphi ’ s rock will witness it . If but my allies

sections.label: Section 1000
sections.original: μείνωσιν ὅρκοι Πυθικὴν ἀνὰ χθόνα ,
sections.translation: in the Pythian land abide by their oaths , this same murderer of his mother will show that no one else shall marry thee my rightful bride . [*] To his cost will he demand satisfaction of King Phoebus for his father ’ s blood ; nor shall his repentance avail him , though he is now submitting to the god .

sections.label: Section 1005
sections.original: ἀλλ ’ ἔκ τ ’ ἐκείνου διαβολαῖς τε ταῖς ἐμαῖς
sections.translation: No ! he shall perish miserably by Apollo ’ s hand and my fake accusations ; so shall he end out my enmity . For the deity upsets the fortune of them that hate him . and suffers them not to be high-minded . Exeunt Orestes and Hermione .

sections.label: Section 1.1009
sections.original: ὦ Φοῖβε πυργώσας τὸν ἐν Ἰλίῳ εὐτειχῆ πάγον
sections.translation: O Phoebus ! who didst fence the hill of Ilium with a fair coronal of towers ,

sections.label: Section 1.1010
sections.original: καὶ πόντιε κυανέαις ἵπποις διφρεύ-
sections.translation: and thou , ocean-god ! coursing o ’ er the main with thy dark steeds , wherefore did ye hand over in dishonour

sections.label: Section 1.1015
sections.original: ἃν χέρα τεκτοσύνας Ἐ-
sections.translation: your own handiwork to the war-god , master of the spear , abandoning Troy to wretchedness ?

sections.label: Section 1.1019
sections.original: πλείστους δ ’ ἐπ ’ ἀκταῖσιν Σιμοεντίσιν εὐίππους
sections.translation: Many a well-horsed car ye yoked on the banks of Simois ,

sections.label: Section 1.1020
sections.original: ἐζεύξατε καὶ φονίους ἀνδρῶν ἁμίλ-
sections.translation: and many a bloody tournament did ye ordain with never a prize to win ; and Ilium ’ s princes are dead and gone ; no longer in Troy

sections.label: Section 1.1025
sections.original: ᾳ θεοῖσιν λέλαμ-
sections.translation: is seen the blaze of fire on altars of the gods with the smoke of incense .

sections.label: Section 2.1027
sections.original: βέβακε δ ’ Ἀτρείδας ἀλόχου παλάμαις ,
sections.translation: The son of Atreus is no more , slain by the hand of his wife , and she herself hath paid the debt of blood by death ,

sections.label: Section 2.1030
sections.original: πρὸς τέκνων ἀπηύρα
sections.translation: and from her children ’ s hands received her doom . The god ’ s own bidding from his oracle was levelled against her , in the day that Agamemnon ’ s son set forth from Argos and visited his shrine ;

sections.label: Section 2.1035
sections.original: κτεάνων , ματρὸς φονεὺς ⋯
sections.translation: so he slew [*] her , aye , spilt his own mother ’ s blood . O Phoebus , O thou power divine , how can I believe the story ?

sections.label: Section 2.1037
sections.original: πολλαὶ δ ’ ἀν ’ Ἑλλάνων ἀγόρους στοναχὰς
sections.translation: Anon wherever Hellenes gather , was heard the voice of lamentation , mothers weeping o ’ er their children ’ s fate ,

sections.label: Section 2.1040
sections.original: ἐξέλειπον οἴκους
sections.translation: as they left their homes to mate with strangers . Ah ! thou art not the only one , nor thy dear ones either , on whom the cloud of grief hath fallen . Hellas had to bear the visitation , and thence the scourge crossed to Phrygia ’ s

sections.label: Section 2.1045
sections.original: καὶ πρὸς εὐκάρπους γύας
sections.translation: fruitful fields , raining the bloody drops the ; death-god loves . [*]

sections.label: Section 1047
sections.original: Φθιώτιδες γυναῖκες , ἱστοροῦντί μοι
sections.translation: Ye dames of Phthia , answer my questions . I heard a vague rumour that the daughter of Menelaus had left these halls

sections.label: Section 1050
sections.original: φρούδη τάδ ’· ἥκω δ ’ ἐκμαθεῖν σπουδὴν ἔχων
sections.translation: and fled ; so now am I come in hot haste to learn if this be true ; for it is the duty of those who are at home to labour in the interests of their absent friends .

sections.label: Section 1053
sections.original: Πηλεῦ , σαφῶς ἤκουσας · οὐδ ’ ἐμοὶ καλὸν
sections.translation: Thou hast heard aright , O Peleus ; ill would it become me to hide the evil case in which I now find myself ;

sections.label: Section 1055
sections.original: βασίλεια γὰρ τῶνδ ’ οἴχεται φυγὰς δόμων .
sections.translation: our queen has fled and left these halls .

sections.label: Section 1056
sections.original: τίνος φόβου τυχοῦσα ; διαπέραινέ μοι .
sections.translation: What was she afraid of ? explain that to me .

sections.label: Section 1057
sections.original: πόσιν τρέμουσα , μὴ δόμων νιν ἐκβάλῃ .
sections.translation: She was fearful her lord would cast her out

sections.label: Section 1058
sections.original: μῶν ἀντὶ παιδὸς θανασίμων βουλευμάτων ;
sections.translation: In return [*] for plotting his child ’ s death ? surely not ?

sections.label: Section 1059
sections.original: ναί , καὶ γυναικὸς αἰχμαλωτίδος φόβῳ .
sections.translation: Yea , and she was afraid of yon captive .

sections.label: Section 1060
sections.original: σὺν πατρὶ δ ’ οἴκους ἢ τίνος λείπει μέτα ;
sections.translation: With whom did she leave the house ? with her father ?

sections.label: Section 1061
sections.original: Ἀγαμέμνονός νιν παῖς βέβηκ ’ ἄγων χθονός .
sections.translation: The son of Agamemnon came and took her hence .
